factual-accuracy.requirement: States Saudi Arabia FLFP rate between 32-37% for 2024-2025 period (national or WDI/ILO estimates)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Saudi Arabia’s female labor force participation rose to “over 34.5% in late 2025,” which falls within the required 32–37% range for the 2024–2025 period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Saudi Arabia FLFP was below 22% pre-2017 before Vision 2030
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Saudi Arabia’s female LFPR as “below 6% in the 1970s and 1980s” and gives other figures (e.g., 5.3% in 1990, 17% in 2017), but it never states that pre-2017 female LFPR was below 22% or ties that explicitly to the period “before Vision 2030.” The specific threshold “below 22% pre-2017” is not asserted.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies 2018 as year of Saudi Arabia driving ban removal
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “Driving ban lifted (2018)” in the Saudi Arabia legal reforms table, identifying 2018 as the year the driving ban was removed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies 2019 as year Saudi women gained passport and travel rights without guardian consent (women 21+)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions Saudi guardianship reforms for travel and work occurring in “2017–2019,” but it does not explicitly identify 2019 as the year women aged 21+ gained passport and travel rights without guardian consent. The specific year and the 21+ age condition are not stated.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names Qurrah childcare subsidy program with amount between 800-1600 SAR per child or per month
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names the Saudi Arabia “Qurrah childcare subsidy” program, but it does not provide any subsidy amount and does not state a figure in the 800–1600 SAR per child/per month range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names Wusool transport subsidy covering approximately 80% of commuting costs
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Saudi childcare support (e.g., Qurrah) but does not mention the Wusool transport subsidy or state that it covers approximately 80% of commuting costs. No transport subsidy program is named with that coverage figure anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States South Korea FLFP rate between 55-63% for 2023-2025 period (accounting for different age bands/definitions)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives South Korea female LFPR as “about 56-57% by 2024-2025” and mentions a 25–54 rate near 73%, but it does not state an FLFP rate specifically for the 2023–2025 period within the 55–63% range. It also does not explicitly frame the 2023–2025 figure as accounting for different age bands/definitions in that range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States South Korea FLFP minimum was approximately 47% in 1998 or late 1990s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides South Korea female LFPR figures for 1970 and 2024–2025 but does not mention FLFP minimum being approximately 47% in 1998 or the late 1990s. There is no statement identifying a late-1990s minimum or giving a 1998 value around 47%.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States South Korea has largest OECD gender wage gap at 29-31% according to 2022 OECD raw wage data, not adjusted for hours/sector
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes South Korea has a large gender wage gap (~31%), but it does not state that South Korea has the largest OECD gender wage gap at 29–31% based on 2022 OECD raw (unadjusted) wage data, nor does it clarify that the figure is not adjusted for hours/sector.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States women hold approximately 46% of paid jobs in South Korea or over 10 million employed women
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides South Korea female labor force participation rates (e.g., ~56–57% by 2024–2025) but does not state that women hold approximately 46% of paid jobs in South Korea, nor does it mention over 10 million employed women. The specific “46% of paid jobs” or “10 million employed women” figure is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States South Korea provides 13 weeks maternity leave and 2-3 weeks paternity leave
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes South Korea’s leave as “18 months combined parental leave in 2025” and “paternity leave increased to 20 days,” but it does not state 13 weeks maternity leave or 2–3 weeks paternity leave. The specific durations required by the criterion are absent and replaced with different figures.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States South Korea achieved universal ECEC by 2013 or early 2010s with center-based care utilization exceeding 70% since 2017
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions South Korea having “Universal ECEC since 2010,” but it does not state universal ECEC was achieved by 2013 or the early 2010s, nor does it mention center-based care utilization exceeding 70% since 2017. The specific timing (2013/early 2010s) and the >70% utilization figure are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes South Korea women age 30-34 employment rate increased from approximately 57% (2013) to 71% (2023)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides overall female LFPR figures for South Korea and mentions age-specific participation patterns, but it does not cite an employment rate for South Korean women aged 30–34 nor the change from ~57% (2013) to 71% (2023). The specific age band (30–34) and the two required year-point values are absent.

The response gives Iceland female LFPR figures like “nearly 70% by 2024 for ages 15+” and “around 88% for women aged 25-54 as of Q2 2025,” but it does not state any 2024–2025 Iceland LFPR rate in the specified ranges (70–72% for 15+, 78–80% for 16–74, or 84–86% for 15–64). The required numeric banded statement is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Iceland 2000 parental leave reform establishing 9 months total with 3 months mother, 3 father, 3 shared structure
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Iceland’s parental leave reform as beginning in 2000 with “3 months non-transferable leave for each parent plus shared months,” matching the 3+3+3 structure. It explicitly states the reform is “Since 2000” and outlines the mother/father non-transferable components and shared portion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Iceland provides 6 months leave per parent, with approximately 4.5 months non-transferable
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Iceland’s parental leave as a total of 12 months with 3 months non-transferable for each parent plus shared months. It does not state that Iceland provides 6 months leave per parent or that approximately 4.5 months are non-transferable, so the specified figures are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Iceland parental leave provides approximately 80% wage replacement up to a cap
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Iceland has “80% salary replacement” for paid parental leave, but it does not mention that this replacement is “up to a cap.” The required capped wage-replacement detail is missing from the Iceland parental leave description.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Iceland fathers' parental leave uptake exceeded 90% by 2006 or remains 85-90%
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes Iceland has “highest paternal leave usage globally (74-90%),” but it does not state that fathers’ uptake exceeded 90% by 2006 or that it remains in the 85–90% range. It also does not mention the year 2006 in connection with Iceland fathers’ leave uptake. The specific threshold-and-timing claim is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies 2017 Iceland equal pay certification requirement for workplaces with 25+ employees
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Iceland’s gender equality framework and parental leave but does not mention the 2017 Iceland equal pay certification law requiring workplaces with 25+ employees to obtain certification. It also does not identify the employee threshold (25+) or the certification requirement as a 2017 policy intervention.

factual-accuracy.requirement: References October 24, 1975 Iceland women's strike with approximately 90% participation as catalyst for gender equality reforms
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Iceland’s high female labor force participation and gender equality laws but does not mention the October 24, 1975 Iceland women’s strike or the approximately 90% participation figure. It also does not frame that strike as a catalyst for subsequent gender equality reforms, which is the required reference.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States approximately 64-66% of Icelandic women work full-time and 34-36% work part-time
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that in Iceland “~59%” of women work part-time, but it does not provide the required split that approximately 64–66% work full-time and 34–36% work part-time. No full-time percentage for Icelandic women is given, and the part-time figure is outside the specified 34–36% range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States India FLFP rate as 32-33% per WDI/ILO modeled estimates for 2024
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives India’s female LFPR as “approximately 35.3% in 2025” and does not state a 2024 WDI/ILO modeled estimate in the 32–33% range. It also does not explicitly attribute any India 2024 figure to WDI/ILO modeled estimates. Therefore the required 32–33% (2024, WDI/ILO) statement is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States India FLFP rate as 41-42% per PLFS usual status for 2023-24
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses India’s female LFPR with various figures (e.g., “peak near 42.7%” and “35.3% in 2025”) but it does not state the India FLFP rate as 41–42% specifically “per PLFS usual status for 2023–24.” The required PLFS usual status framing and the 2023–24 year are not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States India rural FLFP (41-48%) significantly higher than urban FLFP (25-31%) for 2023-24 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that rural female LFPR in India is around 40% and urban around 25%, but it does not specify the required 2023–24 ranges of 41–48% (rural) and 25–31% (urban) nor explicitly compare them as “significantly higher” for that period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States India rural female LFPR increased from approximately 25% (2017-18) to 41-48% (2023-24)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes rural female participation is around 40% and mentions a national low of 23.3% in 2017-18, but it does not state that India rural female LFPR rose from ~25% (2017-18) to 41–48% (2023-24). The specific starting value (~25%) and the 2023-24 range (41–48%) are not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States approximately 85.9% of Indian female workers are in informal employment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states only that “Over 90% of female workers are employed in informal” work in India, but it does not give the specific figure of approximately 85.9% for Indian female workers in informal employment. The exact 85.9% value is never mentioned.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes U-shaped education-participation relationship with lowest FLFP among women with secondary education
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that in India female education follows a U-shaped relationship with labor participation, where middle education levels have the lowest participation, consistent with lowest FLFP among women with secondary education.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides statistics showing male dominance in clerical/sales (e.g., 87-93% male in 2001 census or similar)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses female labor force participation, policies, and sectoral composition across four countries but does not provide any statistics about male dominance in clerical/sales (e.g., 87–93% male in a 2001 census). There is no mention of clerical/sales gender shares or any comparable census-based percentage breakdown. The required male-dominance statistic element is absent.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains how policies interact (e.g., leave paired with childcare capacity; legal reforms requiring enforcement) in at least 2 countries
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses policy interactions in multiple countries, e.g., South Korea’s childcare subsidies tied to parental leave payments and how norms affect paternal uptake, and India’s inheritance reforms where “practice lags” plus mobility/safety constraints reduce impact. It also links Saudi mobility reforms (driving/guardianship) with childcare deficits shaping participation gains.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes participation rates from employment quality (formality, median wages, contract type, benefits coverage) for at least 2 countries
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly separates LFPR trends from employment quality for multiple countries, detailing wage gaps, informality, part-time/nonregular work, and benefits/security issues for South Korea, Iceland, and India (and also notes quality issues for Saudi Arabia). This satisfies the requirement for at least two countries.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes common policy mechanisms or contrasting pathways across countries rather than treating each country in isolation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes an explicit “Comparative Synthesis” section that identifies shared mechanisms (e.g., childcare/leave mediating continuous employment, norms modulating policy effectiveness) and contrasts pathways (e.g., top-down legal reform vs. mature equality model) across the four countries rather than treating them separately.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies next-stage constraints (employer bias, care infrastructure shortfall, occupational segregation) for at least 3 countries
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response identifies next-stage constraints for Saudi Arabia (childcare deficits, segmentation), South Korea (work-family conflict, cultural barriers, precarious jobs), and India (care infrastructure gaps, safety/mobility norms, informality). It also mentions occupational segregation/sectoral concentration across multiple countries and employer-side issues like nonregular employment in Korea.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Analyzes both labor demand factors (job creation, employer practices) and supply factors (skills, mobility, norms) for participation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly separates and discusses supply-side drivers (education, cultural norms, caregiving responsibilities, mobility constraints) and demand-side factors (labor market structure, sector composition, part-time/nonregular work, employer practices like flexible work and sectoral opening). It links these to women’s labor force participation across the four countries.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains how labor market structures (occupational segregation, hours norms, informality, duality) mediate policy effectiveness
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses labor market structures that shape policy impacts, including occupational segregation by sector, part-time versus full-time hours norms (notably Iceland), informality dominating women’s work in India, and labor market segmentation/duality via nonregular employment in South Korea and public/private segmentation in Saudi Arabia, explicitly linking these to policy effectiveness.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes gaps between policy design and real-world uptake/enforcement/firm practice in at least 2 country contexts
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes policy design vs real-world uptake/enforcement gaps in at least two countries: Saudi Arabia (Qurrah childcare subsidy underutilized; guardianship still influential despite reforms) and India (creche mandate mainly benefits formal sector; inheritance rights where practice lags). These are clear examples of design/implementation or enforcement/practice gaps across multiple contexts.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Describes South Korea M-curve age pattern showing female participation peaks in late 20s, dips in 30s during childbearing, then recovers
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response describes South Korea’s “M-shaped” participation curve and explicitly links it to motherhood/childrearing breaks, but it does not clearly state the specific age pattern of peaking in the late 20s, dipping in the 30s during childbearing, and then recovering afterward. The required late-20s peak/30s dip/recovery sequence is not fully articulated.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains that different data sources (WDI/ILO vs national; age bands 15+ vs 16-74 vs 15-64) produce different FLFP figures for same country/period
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that LFPR figures come from ILO/WDI modeled estimates versus national labor force surveys and repeatedly highlights differing age bands (e.g., 15+, 15–64, and country-specific bands like 25–54). It indicates these methodological differences affect comparability of participation rates across sources for the same country/period.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes India FLFP declined from mid-30s in mid-2000s to minimum of 23-26% around 2017-2020
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states that India’s female LFPR declined to a low of 23.3% by 2017–18 after higher levels earlier, which matches the required minimum range of roughly 23–26% around 2017–2020 and implies a fall from much higher (mid-30s+) levels in prior decades.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides detailed analysis for only one or two countries while giving superficial treatment to remaining specified countries
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides substantial, similarly detailed sections for all four countries (Saudi Arabia, South Korea, Iceland, and India), including data trends, policy tables, education/sector/part-time discussion, and demographic projections for each. No country is treated only superficially relative to one or two others.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses scholarly analytical tone with precise labor economics terminology (FLFP, LFPR, labor force participation)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a scholarly analytical tone and repeatedly employs labor economics terminology, explicitly using “LFPR” throughout and referencing “labor force participation” and “female labor force participation” in multiple sections.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes multi-country analysis with clear sections, headings, or comparison tables
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered sections with clear headings (e.g., “Policy Interventions,” “Supply-Side Drivers”) and includes multiple cross-country comparison tables for childcare, parental leave, legal reforms, and demographic trends.

presentation-quality.requirement: Specifies all statistics with units, years, age bands, and source context (e.g., WDI/ILO 15+ vs PLFS)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reports statistics with explicit units (e.g., “% of female population ages 15+”), specifies years/periods (e.g., 1990, 2017–18, Q2 2025), and includes age-band context (15+, 15–64, 25–54). It also provides source context by naming datasets/surveys (ILO/WDI, PLFS, GASTAT LFS, OECD/FRED) and notes methodological differences.

presentation-quality.requirement: Dates policy interventions with specific years (e.g., "2018 driving ban") rather than vague references like "recently"
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly dates policy interventions with specific years, such as “Driving ban lifted (2018),” “Personal Status Law (2022),” “Parental leave first introduced in 1995,” “Since 2000” for Iceland leave, and “Maternity Benefit Act… amended 2017.” These are specific-year references rather than vague timing.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents multi-country data as undifferentiated prose paragraphs without country sections or comparison tables
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized with clear country subsections (e.g., “1.1 Saudi Arabia,” “1.2 South Korea,” etc.) and includes multiple comparison tables (childcare subsidies, parental leave, legal reforms, demographics). It therefore does not present the multi-country data as undifferentiated prose without country sections or tables.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates identical policy conclusions across multiple country sections without country-specific adaptation or examples
presentation-quality.explanation: The response provides distinct, country-specific policy details and examples (e.g., Saudi driving ban lift and guardianship reforms, Korea’s hojuje abolition, Iceland’s non-transferable leave design, India’s Hindu Succession Act changes). While it includes a brief “Commonalities” synthesis, it does not simply repeat identical conclusions across country sections.

citation-quality.requirement: References World Bank WDI, ILO modeled estimates, or OECD data for cross-country comparable labor force participation rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites “International Labour Organization (ILO) Modelled Estimates (ILOEST) database” and “World Bank World Development Indicators,” and also references OECD data sources (e.g., OECD/FRED and OECD reports) for participation rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References national statistics for 3+ of 4 countries (GASTAT/Saudi Arabia, PLFS/India, Statistics Iceland, Korea)
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites national statistics sources for Saudi Arabia (GASTAT), Iceland (Statistics Iceland), and India (PLFS). It also references South Korea’s national statistics via Statistics Korea/KOSIS, meeting the 3+ of 4 requirement.

citation-quality.requirement: References official policy initiatives by name with years (Vision 2030, Qurrah, Wusool, equal pay certification, parental leave reforms)
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly names Saudi policy initiatives including “Vision 2030” and the “Qurrah” childcare subsidy, and it includes years for Vision 2030-era reforms (e.g., post-2017). However, it does not mention “Wusool” or “equal pay certification” by name, and it does not name “parental leave reforms” as a titled initiative.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites personal blogs, Reddit, or Wikipedia as primary sources for FLFP statistics or policy program details
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Wikipedia as a source for policy program/legal detail (reference [46] on the hojuje system). It does not cite Reddit or personal blogs, but Wikipedia is included among the sources used for program details.
